3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills

response.value: Now adding a short TODO entry to track this task.

Progress: I've read the Input README and created a short TODO. Next: here's the concise list of data types recorded in the ComExDBM_CONUS Input files.

- **Fire detections & summaries:** fire mask classes, pixel quality, maximum fire radiative power (MaxFRP), fire pixel position, daily summaries (mean/max MaxFRP, total fire hotspots).
- **Burned-area / fire events:** MODIS MCD64A1 burned area and FIRED-derived fire events/daily burned area.
- **Temperature:** CPC Global Unified daily gridded temperature (daily temperature anomalies) used for heatwave detection.
- **Drought indices:** gridMET-derived indices such as SPI, SPEI, PDSI, and other drought-related metrics at multiple time scales.
- **Meteorological variables:** NARR (e.g., surface temperature, 3‑hr precipitation, soil moisture, relative humidity, specific humidity, latent/sensible heat fluxes, winds at standard pressure levels) and other reanalysis fields.
- **Aerosols & atmospheric composition:** MERRA-2 carbonaceous aerosols (black carbon + organic carbon) and related atmospheric variables (humidity, winds at multiple levels).
- **Vegetation indices:** Leaf Area Index (LAI), Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI).
- **Fire Weather Index (FWI):** daily fire weather index fields from global FWI datasets.

Would you like these grouped into a one-line CSV or exported to a new file in the repo?

children.text: This readme file was created by by Xinming Lin. Last updated on 09/27/2024
Access by contacting Xinming Lin at xinming.lin@pnnl.gov
Project Name: Compound Extremes Data Benchmarking (ComExDBM)
The ComExDBM data product is produced using fires, heatwaves(HWs), droughts and meteorological
data collected through different sources. Here is the detailed description for each data source:
1)  Fire data from the Terra Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS)
a) MODIS Thermal Anomalies and Fire Daily (MOD14A1) Version 6
The datasets are generated at ~1 kilometer (km) spatial resolution and daily temporal resolution.
The variables include the fire mask, pixel quality indicators, maximum fire radiative power (MaxFRP),
and the position of the fire pixel within the scan. Individual 1-km pixels (grids) are assigned to
one of nine fire mask pixel classes, which indicate the different confidence levels of fire occurrence.
In this study, we only use the fire pixels (grids) with the highest confidence level of fire occurrence
to summarize the daily fire features which include mean and maximum of MaxFRP, total number of fire hotspots (FHS).
An open, flexible algorithm & database of U.S. fire events derived from the MODIS burned area product - MCD64A1 (2001-19).
FIREDpy: Python Command Line Interface for classifying fire events from MODIS MCD64A1 Version 6
MCD64A1 is a burned area product generated at 500m spatial resolution and monthly temporal resolution.
The Fire Events Delineation (FIRED), an event-delineation algorithm, has been used to derive the daily fire
burned area from the MODIS MCD64 burned area product for the coterminous US (CONUS) from 2001 to 2020.
The gauge-based Climate Prediction Center (CPC) Global Unified daily gridded temperature data are
provided by NOAA Physical Sciences Laboratory.The data is global telecommunications system (GTS) data
and is gridded using the Shepard Algorithm. The spatial resolution of this data is 0.5°×0.5°(~55km×55km)
All HW events in this study are summarized using the CPC Global Unified daily temperature anomaly data.
CPC Global Unified Temperature can be downloaded at https://psl.noaa.gov/data/gridded/data.cpc.globaltemp.html
3)  Drought data from Gridded Surface Meteorological (gridMET)
The drought indices are obtained from Gridded Surface Meteorological (gridMET) dataset with 4-km spatial resolution
and 5-day temporal resolution. The drought indices provided include:
The SPI and SPEI indices are supplied on different time scales corresponding to the time aggregation of precipitation,
reference evapotranspiration, and precipitation minus reference evapotranspiration, respectively.
The drought indices gridMET can be downloaded at https://www.climatologylab.org/gridmet.html
a) Meteorological variables from the North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR)
Meteorological variables from NARR is available every 3-hour at 32-km horizontal grid spacing and 45 vertical layers
Variables from NARR are showned as follows:
surface temperature, precipitation (P3h) , soil moisture (SM), relative humidity (RH), specific humidity (Qv),
latent heat flux (LHF), and sensible heat flux (SH) at 250, 850 hPa and 100 hPa
The NARR data can be downloaded at https://psl.noaa.gov/data/gridded/data.narr.html
b) Black carbon and organic carbon aerosols (BC+OC) from the Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2)
Meteorological variables from MERRA-2 is available every 3-hour at an approximate spatial resolution of 0.5° × 0.625°
Variables from MERRA-2 are showned as follows:
relative humidity (RH), U-wind and V-wind at 250, 500, and 850 hPa,
carbonaceous aerosols, i.e., black carbon and organic carbon aerosols (BC+OC)
The MERRA-2 data can be downloaded at https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/datasets/M2I3NPASM_5.12.4/summary
Leaf area index (LAI) , normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) from NOAA climate data record (CDR)
LAI and NDVI are available daily at 0.05° x 0.05°
Fire weather index (FWI) from global fire weather database (GFWED)
FWI has  a spatial resolution of 0.5° × 0.625° and daily temporal resolution
Giglio, L., C. Justice. MOD14A1 MODIS/Terra Thermal Anomalies/Fire Daily L3 Global
1km SIN Grid V006. 2015, distributed by NASA EOSDIS Land Processes DAAC,
Giglio, L., C. Justice, L. Boschetti, D. Roy. MCD64A1 MODIS/Terra+Aqua Burned Area
Monthly L3 Global 500m SIN Grid V006. 2015, distributed by NASA EOSDIS Land Processes
Balch, Jennifer K., Lise A. St. Denis, Adam L. Mahood, Nathan P. Mietkiewicz,
Travis M. Williams, Joe McGlinchy, and Maxwell C. Cook. "FIRED (Fire Events Delineation):
an open, flexible algorithm and database of US fire events derived from the MODIS burned

Skills provide specialized capabilities, domain knowledge, and refined workflows for producing high-quality outputs. Each skill folder contains tested instructions for specific domains like testing strategies, API design, or performance optimization. Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.
BLOCKING REQUIREMENT: When a skill applies to the user's request, you MUST load and read the SKILL.md file IMMEDIATELY as your first action, BEFORE generating any other response or taking action on the task. Use #tool:readFile to load the relevant skill(s).
NEVER just mention or reference a skill in your response without actually reading it first. If a skill is relevant, load it before proceeding.
How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request
2. If any skill's domain overlaps with the task, load that skill immediately
3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills
Examples:
- "Help me write unit tests for this module" -> Load the testing skill via #tool:readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Optimize this slow function" -> Load the performance-profiling skill via #tool:readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Add a discount code field to checkout" -> Load both the checkout-flow and form-validation skills FIRST

<name>agent-customization</name>
<description>**WORKFLOW SKILL** — Create, update, review, fix, or debug VS Code agent customization files (.instructions.md, .prompt.md, .agent.md, SKILL.md, copilot-instructions.md, AGENTS.md). USE FOR: saving coding preferences; troubleshooting why instructions/skills/agents are ignored or not invoked; configuring applyTo patterns; defining tool restrictions; creating custom agent modes or specialized workflows; packaging domain knowledge; fixing YAML frontmatter syntax. INVOKES: file system tools (read/write customization files), ask-questions tool (interview user for requirements), subagents for codebase exploration. FOR SINGLE OPERATIONS: For quick YAML frontmatter fixes or creating a single file from a known pattern, edit the file directly — no skill needed.</description>

response.value:
Summarizing how the project data is organized and structured.

- **Top-level layout:** repository root contains the
project folder (

).  
- **Raw inputs:**

(see README) — raw/source datasets organized by data provider/type (MODIS MOD14A1, MODIS MCD64A1 / FIRED, CPC temperature, gridMET drought indices, NARR, MERRA‑2, LAI/NDVI, Global FWI). Also contains spatial masks/shapefiles in `CONUS4326`.  
- **Processing code:**

— Python scripts used to create summaries, label events, match fires/heatwaves, compute drought metrics, and save outputs.  
- **Analysis & intermediate products:**

— intermediate NetCDFs and analysis figures (daily/annual aggregates, maps, and plot folders).  
- **Final outputs:**

— processed ComExDBM products organized by theme (e.g., `01_HeatWave`, `02_FireData`, `03_DroughtData`, `04_Meteorological_Variables`), typically as per-year NetCDF files named like `ComExDBM_YYYY_HeatWave_V02.nc` and similar.  
- **Per-source structure & metadata:** each input source is kept with its native spatial/temporal resolution and documented in the input README (examples: MOD14A1 — daily ~1 km; MCD64A1 — monthly 500 m with FIRED-derived daily burned-area; CPC — 0.5° daily anomalies; gridMET — 4 km drought indices at multi-day aggregations; NARR/MERRA‑2 — 3‑hour reanalysis fields; LAI/NDVI — daily 0.05°; FWI — daily 0.5°×0.625°).  
- **Naming & summary convention:** processed outputs are grouped by hazard type and year, with summary variables (e.g., mean/max MaxFRP, FHS counts, drought indices, heatwave summaries) stored in the NetCDFs under `04_OutputData`.

Would you like me to create a short CSV inventory of input sources and resolutions in the repo?

Summarizing how the project data is organized and structured.

- **Top-level layout:** repository root contains the `ComExDBM_CONUS` project folder ([ComExDBM_CONUS](ComExDBM_CONUS)).  
- **Raw inputs:** [ComExDBM_CONUS/01_Input](ComExDBM_CONUS/01_Input) (see [README](ComExDBM_CONUS/01_Input/README.md)) — raw/source datasets organized by data provider/type (MODIS MOD14A1, MODIS MCD64A1 / FIRED, CPC temperature, gridMET drought indices, NARR, MERRA‑2, LAI/NDVI, Global FWI). Also contains spatial masks/shapefiles in `CONUS4326`.  
- **Processing code:** [ComExDBM_CONUS/02_Code](ComExDBM_CONUS/02_Code) — Python scripts used to create summaries, label events, match fires/heatwaves, compute drought metrics, and save outputs.  
- **Analysis & intermediate products:** [ComExDBM_CONUS/03_Analysis](ComExDBM_CONUS/03_Analysis) — intermediate NetCDFs and analysis figures (daily/annual aggregates, maps, and plot folders).  
- **Final outputs:** [ComExDBM_CONUS/04_OutputData](ComExDBM_CONUS/04_OutputData) — processed ComExDBM products organized by theme (e.g., `01_HeatWave`, `02_FireData`, `03_DroughtData`, `04_Meteorological_Variables`), typically as per-year NetCDF files named like `ComExDBM_YYYY_HeatWave_V02.nc` and similar.  
- **Per-source structure & metadata:** each input source is kept with its native spatial/temporal resolution and documented in the input README (examples: MOD14A1 — daily ~1 km; MCD64A1 — monthly 500 m with FIRED-derived daily burned-area; CPC — 0.5° daily anomalies; gridMET — 4 km drought indices at multi-day aggregations; NARR/MERRA‑2 — 3‑hour reanalysis fields; LAI/NDVI — daily 0.05°; FWI — daily 0.5°×0.625°).  
- **Naming & summary convention:** processed outputs are grouped by hazard type and year, with summary variables (e.g., mean/max MaxFRP, FHS counts, drought indices, heatwave summaries) stored in the NetCDFs under `04_OutputData`.

Would you like me to create a short CSV inventory of input sources and resolutions in the repo?

Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.

NEVER just mention or reference a skill in your response without actually reading it first. If a skill is relevant, load it before proceeding.
How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request
2. If any skill's domain overlaps with the task, load that skill immediately
3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills
Examples:
- "Help me write unit tests for this module" -> Load the testing skill via #tool:readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Optimize this slow function" -> Load the performance-profiling skill via #tool:readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Add a discount code field to checkout" -> Load both the checkout-flow and form-validation skills FIRST

First, I need to focus on model selection, considering probabilistic models like Gradient Boosting or Bayesian models, and possibly ensembles. I’ll also forecast lead times of 7 and 14 days using the relevant predictors. Then, calibration and evaluation metrics will include the Brier score and reliability diagrams. I'll need to address explainability using SHAP and operationalize with real-time pipelines. Finally, I’ll set up a todo list using the manage_todo_list tool and provide a progress update afterward.

The final formatting calls for bold headers in Title Case, but I’ll avoid heavy formatting. I won't volunteer specific model names, as that’s not required. **Structuring ML Drivers and Approach**

I’m considering drivers like SHAP values per region and time, alongside spatial maps of feature importance. I could mention methods like Granger causality and uplift regression but need to keep it concise. There’s the idea of using multi-task learning to predict events like heatwaves or wildfires jointly, modeling dependencies via techniques like copulas. I should also propose approaches for joint probabilities using classifiers. I also need to manage the todo list call, acknowledging it will overwrite earlier entries.

response.value: I'll add a concise TODO plan for the ML workflow, then outline the modeling approach and steps.

todoList.title: Explainability and regional driver analysis (SHAP etc.)

response.value:
Progress: created a 9‑step TODO for the ML workflow. Next I'll summarize the modeling design and concrete steps.

Plan summary — how I'd estimate 1–2 week compound‑hazard probabilities and identify drivers

1) Define targets
- Binary/multi‑label targets per grid cell/region for lead times 7 and 14 days: Heatwave (HW), Drought (DR), Wildfire occurrence (WF).  
- Compound events: joint label for co‑occurrence (HW & DR), and WF during HW/DR. Options: (a) direct multi‑label classifier for joint events; (b) model marginals and estimate joint probability via P(A∩B)=P(A)·P(B|A) with a conditional model.

2) Data assembly & preprocessing
- Use inputs from

(MOD14A1, MCD64A1/FIRED, CPC temperature, gridMET indices, NARR/MERRA‑2, LAI/NDVI, FWI).  
- Regrid / resample to a common spatial grid (choose resolution balancing signal vs compute; e.g., 0.05° or 4 km). Align temporal resolution to daily and aggregate where needed (3‑hr → daily). Fill missing with conservative imputation and flag gaps.  
- Create target times by labeling each grid cell at day t with event occurrence in window [t+7] and [t+14].

3) Features & engineering
- Lagged predictors: rolling means/sums (1–14, 7–28 days) of temperature anomalies, precipitation, SPI/SPEI, PDSI, soil moisture, MaxFRP, FWI, LAI/NDVI, humidity, winds, aerosol (BC+OC).  
- Anomalies & climatology: standardized anomalies relative to seasonal cycles.  
- Derived indices: recent trends, day‑of‑year, persistence features, antecedent fire counts.  
- Include spatial context: neighborhood aggregates (e.g., 3×3 cell mean), elevation/landcover/eco‑region masks, distance to recent fires.  
- Optionally include NWP/forecast features if available (ensemble means) to extend predictive skill for 7–14 days.

4) Modeling approach
- Baselines: persistence, climatology, logistic regression.  
- Primary models (probabilistic): Gradient-boosted trees with probabilistic outputs (LightGBM / XGBoost / CatBoost using a logistic objective), and probabilistic neural nets (e.g., MC‑dropout or deep ensembles) for complex nonlinear interactions.  
- Multi‑output strategies: (a) single model predicting joint probability directly, (b) multi-task model predicting marginals + conditional, (c) classifier chain.  
- Handle class imbalance with class weights, focal loss, or up/down sampling.

5) Validation & CV
- Time‑aware splits: train on past years, validate on later years. Use rolling origin evaluation for lead times.  
- Spatial CV: leave‑region‑out folds to test generalization across regions.  
- Metrics: Brier score (main for probabilistic), CRPS (if continuous forecasts), ROC AUC, precision‑recall, reliability diagrams, and sharpness. Compare against persistence/climatology.

6) Calibration & uncertainty
- Calibrate probabilities (Platt / isotonic / beta calibration).  
- Quantify uncertainty via probabilistic ensembles, Bayesian models, or prediction intervals (for neural nets use ensembles or MC dropout).  
- Produce lead‑time dependent reliability diagnostics.

7) Explainability & regional drivers
- Use SHAP (TreeSHAP for boosted trees) to get local and global feature attributions. Aggregate SHAP by region, season, and lead time to identify dominant drivers (e.g., antecedent soil moisture, positive temp anomaly, high FWI, low LAI).  
- Map spatial patterns of feature importance and partial dependence plots to show nonlinear effects.  
- Use interaction detection (SHAP interaction values) to find combinations (e.g., high temp anomaly + low soil moisture → elevated joint risk).  
- Cluster events/regimes (k‑means or spectral clustering on features) to define regional modes and their drivers.

8) Postprocessing & deployment
- Produce gridded probabilistic maps for 7 and 14‑day leads and regional summaries.  
- Build an ingestion pipeline to update with newest reanalysis/NWP and retrain periodically. Containerize model + inference.  
- Provide user‑facing products: probability maps, reliability diagrams, top‑drivers per region, and alerts when joint probability exceeds thresholds.  
- Log performance and drift; set automated revalidation.

9) Tests / case studies
- Backtest against historical compound events (2001–2020) and perform event‑based evaluation (hit rate, false alarms) and prospective case studies (major heat‑drought‑fire years).  
- Produce interpretability reports for a small set of high‑impact events to validate driver explanations.

Deliverables I would produce
- Reproducible data prep scripts (`02_Code` style), training notebooks, and saved model artifacts.  
- Evaluation dashboards (Brier, reliability, AUC) and SHAP‑based driver maps.  
- A short README describing model inputs, versions, and recommended operational thresholds.

If you want, I can:
- (A) scaffold the reproducible pipeline (data‑prep + feature engineering scripts) under

, or  
- (B) prototype a baseline LightGBM model for 7‑day joint HW+DR probability on a small region and produce SHAP driver maps.

Which do you want me to start?

- Regrid / resample to a common spatial grid (choose resolution balancing signal vs compute; e.g., 0.05° or 4 km). Align temporal resolution to daily and aggregate where needed (3‑hr → daily). Fill missing with conservative imputation and flag gaps.  
- Create target times by labeling each grid cell at day t with event occurrence in window [t+7] and [t+14].

3) Features & engineering
- Lagged predictors: rolling means/sums (1–14, 7–28 days) of temperature anomalies, precipitation, SPI/SPEI, PDSI, soil moisture, MaxFRP, FWI, LAI/NDVI, humidity, winds, aerosol (BC+OC).  
- Anomalies & climatology: standardized anomalies relative to seasonal cycles.  
- Derived indices: recent trends, day‑of‑year, persistence features, antecedent fire counts.  
- Include spatial context: neighborhood aggregates (e.g., 3×3 cell mean), elevation/landcover/eco‑region masks, distance to recent fires.  
- Optionally include NWP/forecast features if available (ensemble means) to extend predictive skill for 7–14 days.

4) Modeling approach
- Baselines: persistence, climatology, logistic regression.  
- Primary models (probabilistic): Gradient-boosted trees with probabilistic outputs (LightGBM / XGBoost / CatBoost using a logistic objective), and probabilistic neural nets (e.g., MC‑dropout or deep ensembles) for complex nonlinear interactions.  
- Multi‑output strategies: (a) single model predicting joint probability directly, (b) multi-task model predicting marginals + conditional, (c) classifier chain.  
- Handle class imbalance with class weights, focal loss, or up/down sampling.

5) Validation & CV
- Time‑aware splits: train on past years, validate on later years. Use rolling origin evaluation for lead times.  
- Spatial CV: leave‑region‑out folds to test generalization across regions.  
- Metrics: Brier score (main for probabilistic), CRPS (if continuous forecasts), ROC AUC, precision‑recall, reliability diagrams, and sharpness. Compare against persistence/climatology.

6) Calibration & uncertainty
- Calibrate probabilities (Platt / isotonic / beta calibration).  
- Quantify uncertainty via probabilistic ensembles, Bayesian models, or prediction intervals (for neural nets use ensembles or MC dropout).  
- Produce lead‑time dependent reliability diagnostics.

7) Explainability & regional drivers
- Use SHAP (TreeSHAP for boosted trees) to get local and global feature attributions. Aggregate SHAP by region, season, and lead time to identify dominant drivers (e.g., antecedent soil moisture, positive temp anomaly, high FWI, low LAI).  
- Map spatial patterns of feature importance and partial dependence plots to show nonlinear effects.  
- Use interaction detection (SHAP interaction values) to find combinations (e.g., high temp anomaly + low soil moisture → elevated joint risk).  
- Cluster events/regimes (k‑means or spectral clustering on features) to define regional modes and their drivers.

8) Postprocessing & deployment
- Produce gridded probabilistic maps for 7 and 14‑day leads and regional summaries.  
- Build an ingestion pipeline to update with newest reanalysis/NWP and retrain periodically. Containerize model + inference.  
- Provide user‑facing products: probability maps, reliability diagrams, top‑drivers per region, and alerts when joint probability exceeds thresholds.  
- Log performance and drift; set automated revalidation.

9) Tests / case studies
- Backtest against historical compound events (2001–2020) and perform event‑based evaluation (hit rate, false alarms) and prospective case studies (major heat‑drought‑fire years).  
- Produce interpretability reports for a small set of high‑impact events to validate driver explanations.

Deliverables I would produce
- Reproducible data prep scripts (`02_Code` style), training notebooks, and saved model artifacts.  
- Evaluation dashboards (Brier, reliability, AUC) and SHAP‑based driver maps.  
- A short README describing model inputs, versions, and recommended operational thresholds.

If you want, I can:
- (A) scaffold the reproducible pipeline (data‑prep + feature engineering scripts) under `ComExDBM_CONUS/02_Code`, or  
- (B) prototype a baseline LightGBM model for 7‑day joint HW+DR probability on a small region and produce SHAP driver maps.

Which do you want me to start?
